title.zh: 卷五十四 李廣蘇建傳
title.en: Volume 54: Li Guang and Su Jian
title.raw: 卷五十四 李廣蘇建傳 Volume 54: Li Guang and Su Jian

sentences.zh: 李廣，隴西成紀人也。

translations.literal: Li Guang was a man of Chengji in Longxi.
translations.idiomatic: Li Guang came from Chengji in Longxi commandery.

sentences.zh: 其先曰李信，秦時為將，逐得燕太子丹者也。

translations.literal: His ancestor was called Li Xin; in Qin times he was a general—the one who pursued and captured Crown Prince Dan of Yan.
translations.idiomatic: His forebear Li Xin had served as a Qin general and was the officer who ran down and captured the crown prince of Yan, Dan.

sentences.zh: 廣世世受射。

translations.literal: For generations the Guang family received instruction in archery.
translations.idiomatic: Archery had been handed down in Li Guang's family for generations.

sentences.zh: 孝文十四年，匈奴大入蕭關，而廣以良家子從軍擊胡，用善射，殺首虜多，為郎，騎常侍。

translations.literal: In the fourteenth year of Emperor Xiaowen's reign the Xiongnu entered in force at Xiaoguan, and Guang, as a son of a respectable household, joined the army to strike the Hu; because he was skilled at archery and killed many enemy heads and captives, he was made a gentleman-attendant and cavalry attendant-in-ordinary.
translations.idiomatic: In 166 BCE the Xiongnu poured through Xiaoguan. Li Guang enlisted as a young man of good family, fought them with the army, and distinguished himself with the bow—so many kills that he was appointed gentleman-attendant and then cavalry attendant-in-ordinary at court.

sentences.zh: 數從射獵，格殺猛獸，文帝曰：「惜廣不逢時，令當高祖世，萬戶侯豈足道哉！」

translations.literal: Repeatedly he accompanied [the emperor] on hunts and shoots, grappling with and killing fierce beasts. Emperor Wen said, "It is a pity Guang does not meet his season; had he lived in the age of Gaozu, a marquisate of ten thousand households—how could that be worth mentioning!"
translations.idiomatic: He often rode out on the hunt with the emperor and killed dangerous game at close quarters. Emperor Wen remarked, "What a pity Li Guang was not born in Gaozu's day—a mere marquisate of ten thousand households would have been beneath him."

sentences.zh: 匈奴侵上郡，上使中貴人從廣勒習兵擊匈奴。

translations.literal: The Xiongnu invaded Shang commandery; the emperor sent a palace favorite to follow Guang in drilling troops to strike the Xiongnu.
translations.idiomatic: When the Xiongnu raided Shang commandery, the emperor dispatched a eunuch favorite to train under Li Guang and practice campaigning against them.

sentences.zh: 中貴人者數十騎從，見匈奴三人，與戰。

translations.literal: The palace favorite had several dozen horsemen following him; they saw three Xiongnu and fought them.
translations.idiomatic: The favorite rode out with a few dozen cavalry, encountered three Xiongnu horsemen, and engaged them.

sentences.zh: 射傷中貴人，殺其騎且盡。

translations.literal: They shot and wounded the palace favorite and killed his riders nearly to the last man.
translations.idiomatic: The Xiongnu wounded the favorite with their bows and all but wiped out his escort.

sentences.zh: 中貴人走廣，廣曰：「是必射鵰者也。」

translations.literal: The palace favorite fled to Guang; Guang said, "These must be men who shoot the great eagle."
translations.idiomatic: The favorite galloped to Li Guang, who said, "Those were Xiongnu eagle-archers—men who can bring down a golden eagle on the wing."

sentences.zh: 廣乃從百騎往馳三人。

translations.literal: Guang then took a hundred riders and galloped after the three men.
translations.idiomatic: Li Guang took a hundred horsemen and rode hard after the three.

sentences.zh: 三人亡馬步行，行數十里。

translations.literal: The three had lost their horses and walked on foot for several tens of li.
translations.idiomatic: They had lost their mounts and were walking—already many li from where they had fought.

sentences.zh: 廣令其騎張左右翼，而廣身自射彼三人者，殺其二人，生得一人，果匈奴射鵰者也。

translations.literal: Guang ordered his riders to spread the left and right wings, while Guang himself shot at those three men, killed two of them, and captured one alive; they proved indeed to be Xiongnu eagle-archers.
translations.idiomatic: Li Guang fanned his men to left and right, then shot the three himself—two dead, one taken alive—and they turned out to be the eagle-archers after all.

sentences.zh: 已縛之上山，望匈奴數千騎，見廣，以為誘騎，驚，上山陳。

translations.literal: Having bound him, they went up a hill; looking out they saw several thousand Xiongnu riders. When they saw Guang, they took them for bait cavalry, were alarmed, and went up the hill to form battle array.
translations.idiomatic: They had tied up their prisoner and climbed a rise when they sighted thousands of enemy horse. The Xiongnu mistook Li Guang's tiny band for a decoy, hesitated, and drew up on the heights in formation.

sentences.zh: 廣之百騎皆大恐，欲馳還走。

translations.literal: Guang's hundred riders were all greatly frightened and wished to gallop back in flight.
translations.idiomatic: His hundred men panicked and wanted to wheel and run for it.

sentences.zh: 廣曰：「我去大軍數十里，今如此走，匈奴追射，我立盡。

translations.literal: Guang said, "We are several tens of li from the main army; if we flee like this now, the Xiongnu will pursue and shoot us—we will be finished at once.
translations.idiomatic: Li Guang said, "We are dozens of li from the main force. If we bolt now, they'll run us down and pick us off—we'll be dead in moments.

sentences.zh: 今我留，匈奴必以我為大軍之誘，不我擊。」

translations.literal: If we now remain, the Xiongnu are sure to take us for bait for a large army and will not strike us."
translations.idiomatic: If we stay put, they'll assume we're bait for a larger ambush and hold their fire."

sentences.zh: 廣令曰：「前！」

translations.literal: Guang ordered, "Forward!"
translations.idiomatic: Li Guang gave the order: "Advance!"

sentences.zh: 未到匈奴陳二里所，止，令曰：「皆下馬解鞍！」

translations.literal: When they had not yet reached within about two li of the Xiongnu formation, he halted and ordered, "All dismount and unstrap the saddles!"
translations.idiomatic: They rode to within about two li of the enemy line, then he halted the column and shouted, "Dismount—loosen every saddle girth!"

sentences.zh: 騎曰：「虜多如是，解鞍，即急，奈何？」

translations.literal: The riders said, "The enemy are so many; if we unstrap the saddles, when it becomes urgent, what then?"
translations.idiomatic: His men protested: "There are so many of them—if we take off our saddles and things turn ugly, what then?"

sentences.zh: 廣曰：「彼虜以我為走，今解鞍以示不去，用堅其意。」

translations.literal: Guang said, "Those barbarians take us for men who will flee; now by unstrapping the saddles we show we are not going away, thereby to make their judgment firm."
translations.idiomatic: Li Guang replied, "They think we're running. Dismounting and stripping the gear shows we mean to stay—that will fix their idea that we're only a screen for something bigger."

sentences.zh: 有白馬將出護兵。

translations.literal: There was a white-horse general who came out to oversee the troops.
translations.idiomatic: A Xiongnu officer on a white horse rode out to supervise his men.

sentences.zh: 廣上馬，與十餘騎奔射殺白馬將，而復還至其百騎中，解鞍，縱馬臥。

translations.literal: Guang mounted his horse; with more than ten riders he galloped and shot, killing the white-horse general, then returned again to his hundred riders, unstrapped the saddles, and let the horses lie down.
translations.idiomatic: Li Guang vaulted into the saddle, took a dozen riders in a dash, killed the white-horse commander with a volley, then cantered back to his line, loosened the saddles again, and let the horses graze and lie down as before.

sentences.zh: 時會暮，胡兵終怪之，弗敢擊。

translations.literal: It happened to be dusk; the Hu troops in the end found it strange and did not dare attack.
translations.idiomatic: Dusk was falling; the enemy remained baffled by the display and still would not close.

sentences.zh: 夜半，胡兵以為漢有伏軍於傍欲夜取之，即引去。

translations.literal: In the middle of the night the Hu troops thought the Han had ambush troops at the side intending to take them by night, and so withdrew.
translations.idiomatic: Around midnight they decided Han ambushers must be waiting to strike in the dark, and broke camp.

sentences.zh: 平旦，廣乃歸其大軍。

translations.literal: At daybreak Guang then returned to his main army.
translations.idiomatic: At first light Li Guang rejoined the main column.

sentences.zh: 後徙為隴西、北地、雁門中雲中太守。

translations.literal: Afterward he was transferred to be governor of Longxi, Beidi, Yanmen, Dai, and Yunzhong.
translations.idiomatic: He was later posted in turn as governor of Longxi, Beidi, Yanmen, Dai commandery, and Yunzhong along the northern frontier.

sentences.zh: 後漢誘單于以馬邑城，使大軍伏馬邑傍，而廣為驍騎將軍，屬護軍將軍。

translations.literal: Afterward the Han lured the Chanyu with Mayi city, had a great army lie in ambush beside Mayi, and Guang was made dashing-cavalry general, subordinate to the protector-general.
translations.idiomatic: Later the court tried to lure the Chanyu into Mayi with a ruse while the main army lay in wait; Li Guang was appointed dashing-cavalry general under the protector-general's command.

sentences.zh: 單于覺之，去，漢軍皆無功。

translations.literal: The Chanyu perceived it and departed; the Han armies all achieved no merit.
translations.idiomatic: The Chanyu saw through the trap and withdrew, and the Han expedition came away with nothing.

sentences.zh: 後四歲，廣以衛尉為將軍，出雁門擊匈奴。

translations.literal: Four years later Guang, as commandant of the guards, became a general and went out through Yanmen to strike the Xiongnu.
translations.idiomatic: Four years after that he left the capital as commandant of the guards, took the title of general, and marched out through Yanmen against the Xiongnu.

sentences.zh: 匈奴兵多，破廣軍，生得廣。

translations.literal: The Xiongnu troops were many; they defeated Guang's army and captured Guang alive.
translations.idiomatic: The enemy outnumbered him, broke his force, and took Li Guang prisoner.

sentences.zh: 單于素聞廣賢，令曰：「得李廣必生致之。」

translations.literal: The Chanyu had long heard that Guang was worthy and ordered, "If Li Guang is taken, he must be brought in alive."
translations.idiomatic: The Chanyu had heard of Li Guang's reputation and issued orders: "Li Guang is to be captured alive."

sentences.zh: 胡騎得廣，廣時傷，置兩馬間。

translations.literal: The Hu riders got Guang; Guang at the time was wounded; they placed him between two horses.
translations.idiomatic: The horsemen had him—wounded—and slung him in a rope cradle between two mounts.

sentences.zh: 絡而盛臥。

translations.literal: They netted him and packed him lying down.
translations.idiomatic: They bound him in a net and carried him along supine between the animals.

sentences.zh: 行十餘里，廣陽死，睨其傍有一兒騎善馬，暫騰而上胡兒馬，因抱兒鞭馬南馳數十里，得其餘軍。

translations.literal: Having gone more than ten li, Guang feigned death; he squinted and saw beside him a youth riding a fine horse; in a moment he leaped up onto the Hu youth's horse, then embracing the youth he whipped the horse and galloped south several tens of li, reaching the rest of his army.
translations.idiomatic: After some ten li he feigned death, spotted a young rider on a fine horse at his side, vaulted onto the animal behind him, locked an arm around the boy to hold him fast, and spurred south for dozens of li until he reached the rest of his troops.

sentences.zh: 匈奴騎數百追之，廣行取兒弓射殺追騎，以故得脫。

translations.literal: Several hundred Xiongnu riders pursued him; Guang along the way took the youth's bow and shot and killed the pursuing riders; by this means he got away.
translations.idiomatic: Hundreds of pursuers closed on him; he snatched the lad's bow as he rode and picked off the leaders until they broke off the chase.

sentences.zh: 於是至漢，漢下廣吏。

translations.literal: Thereupon he reached the Han; the Han handed Guang over to the officials.
translations.idiomatic: Back on Han soil the court turned him over to the judiciary for trial.

sentences.zh: 吏當廣亡失多，為虜所生得，當斬，贖為庶人。

translations.literal: The officials judged Guang for many losses and flight and for being taken alive by the enemy—liable to execution; he ransomed himself and became a commoner.
translations.idiomatic: The judges found him guilty of heavy casualties and capture by the enemy—capital crimes—but he commuted the sentence with a fine and was stripped of rank.

sentences.zh: 數歲，與故穎陰侯屏居藍田南山中射獵。

translations.literal: For several years, together with the former Marquis of Yingyin he lived in retirement in the southern hills of Lantian, hunting with bow.
translations.idiomatic: For some years he lived in seclusion in the Lantian hills with the former Marquis of Yingyin, passing the days hunting.

sentences.zh: 嘗夜從一騎出，從人田間飲。

translations.literal: Once at night he went out with one rider following and drank in someone's field.
translations.idiomatic: One night he rode out with a single attendant and stopped at a farmhouse for wine.

sentences.zh: 還至亭，霸陵尉醉，呵止廣，廣騎曰：「故李將軍。」

translations.literal: Returning they reached a post station; the Baling post captain was drunk and shouted to stop Guang; Guang's rider said, "The former General Li."
translations.idiomatic: On the way back they were halted at a courier station by a drunken captain of Baling, who challenged them; Li Guang's man called out, "That is the former General Li."

sentences.zh: 尉曰：「今將軍尚不得夜行，何故也！」

translations.literal: The captain said, "The present general still may not travel by night—how much more so in your case!"
translations.idiomatic: The captain retorted, "Even an active general is not allowed through here after dark—what business have you?"

sentences.zh: 宿廣亭下。

translations.literal: He lodged Guang beneath the station.
translations.idiomatic: He made Li Guang spend the night under the station eaves.

sentences.zh: 居無何，匈奴入遼西，殺太守，敗韓將軍。

translations.literal: Before long had passed, the Xiongnu entered Liaoxi, killed the governor, and defeated General Han.
translations.idiomatic: Not long afterward the Xiongnu struck Liaoxi, killed the governor there, and routed General Han Anguo.

sentences.zh: 韓將軍後徙居右北平，死。

translations.literal: General Han afterward was transferred to reside at Youbeiping and died.
translations.idiomatic: General Han was later transferred to Youbeiping, where he died in office.

sentences.zh: 於是上乃召拜廣為右北平太守。

translations.literal: Thereupon the emperor summoned and appointed Guang governor of Youbeiping.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor then recalled Li Guang and named him governor of Youbeiping.

sentences.zh: 廣請霸陵尉與俱，至軍而斬之，上書自陳謝罪。

translations.literal: Guang requested the Baling post captain to go with him; when they reached the army he beheaded him, and submitted a memorial stating himself and apologizing for his crime.
translations.idiomatic: Li Guang insisted that the Baling captain accompany him to headquarters and had him executed on arrival, then filed a memorial explaining himself and asking to be punished.

sentences.zh: 上報曰：「將軍者，國之爪牙也。

translations.literal: The emperor replied in a rescript, "The general is the claws and fangs of the state.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor answered: "A general is the fangs and claws of the realm.

sentences.zh: 《司馬法》曰：『登車不式，遭喪不服，振旅撫師，以征不服，率三軍之心，同戰士之力，故怒形則千里竦，威振則萬物狀；

translations.literal: The Sima fa says: 'Mount the chariot without saluting; meet mourning without donning mourning garments; rouse the army and comfort the hosts to punish those who do not submit; lead the hearts of the three armies and unite the strength of the fighting men—therefore when anger appears, a thousand li are awed; when might shakes forth, the myriad things take form;
translations.idiomatic: The Military Canons of Sima say: 'Board your chariot without bowing in courtesy; on campaign set mourning aside; marshal the host and steady the ranks to chastise rebellion; bind the three armies to one purpose and the soldiery to one will—then a flash of wrath makes the realm tremble for a thousand li, and the sweep of your majesty gives shape to all beneath heaven;

sentences.zh: 是以名聲暴於夷貉，威稜憺乎鄰國。』

translations.literal: thereby fame is displayed among the Yi and Mo, and the edge of awe spreads to neighboring states.'"
translations.idiomatic: so that your name thunders among the barbarians and the glint of your power overawes every neighbor.'"

sentences.zh: 夫報忿除害，捐殘去殺，朕之所圖於將軍也；

translations.literal: As for repaying resentment and removing harm, casting off cruelty and doing away with killing—that is what We intend for the general;
translations.idiomatic: To settle scores with wrongdoers, root out evil, and curb needless bloodshed—that is what I expect of you as a commander;

sentences.zh: 若乃免冠徒跣，稽顙請罪，豈朕之指哉！

translations.literal: but as for taking off the cap, going barefoot, and knocking the forehead in begging forgiveness—is that Our intent!
translations.idiomatic: not for you to bare your head, go barefoot, and beat your brow in the posture of a criminal—that was never my meaning!

sentences.zh: 將軍其率師東轅，彌節白檀，以臨右北平盛秋。」

translations.literal: General, lead your army with east-turning shafts, slow the pace at Baitan, and in the height of autumn come to Youbeiping."
translations.idiomatic: Turn your column east, take up position at Baitan, and be in Youbeiping by the height of autumn."

sentences.zh: 廣在郡，匈奴號曰「漢飛將軍」，避之，數歲不入界。

translations.literal: While Guang was in the commandery, the Xiongnu called him "the Han Flying General," avoided him, and for several years did not enter the border.
translations.idiomatic: While he held the commandery the Xiongnu dubbed him the Flying General of Han and gave his district a wide berth for years.

sentences.zh: 廣出獵，見草中石，以為虎而射之，中石沒矢，視之，石也，他日射之，終不能入矣。

translations.literal: Guang went out hunting; he saw a stone in the grass, thought it a tiger, and shot at it; he hit the stone and the arrow sank in; looking at it, it was a stone; another day he shot at it and in the end could not make it enter.
translations.idiomatic: Once on a hunt he mistook a boulder in tall grass for a tiger and let fly; the shaft buried itself to the fletching in solid rock. When he saw what it was, he tried again on another day and could never repeat the shot.

sentences.zh: 廣所居郡聞有虎，常自射之。

translations.literal: In whatever commandery Guang dwelt, if tigers were heard of, he always shot them himself.
translations.idiomatic: Wherever he was posted, if word came of a man-eater, he went after it himself.

sentences.zh: 及居右北平射虎，虎騰傷廣，廣亦射殺之。

translations.literal: When he dwelt at Youbeiping and shot a tiger, the tiger leaped up and wounded Guang; Guang also shot and killed it.
translations.idiomatic: At Youbeiping a tiger he had wounded sprang and mauled him; he still brought it down with his bow.

sentences.zh: 石建卒，上召廣代為郎中令。

translations.literal: Shi Jian died; the emperor summoned Guang to replace him as superintendent of the palace gentlemen.
translations.idiomatic: When Shi Jian died, the emperor called Li Guang to the capital to succeed him as superintendent of the palace gentlemen.

sentences.zh: 元朔六年，廣復為將軍，從大將軍出定襄。

translations.literal: In the sixth year of Yuanshuo Guang again became a general and followed the supreme commander out of Dingxiang.
translations.idiomatic: In 123 BCE he was again given a general's commission and marched from Dingxiang under the supreme commander Wei Qing.

sentences.zh: 諸將多中首虜率為侯者，而廣軍無功。

translations.literal: Of the various generals many met the head-and-captive quota and became marquises, but Guang's army achieved no merit.
translations.idiomatic: Most of the subordinate commanders earned enough kills to be ennobled; Li Guang's column alone came back empty-handed.

sentences.zh: 後三歲，廣以郎中令將四千騎出右北平，博望侯張騫將萬騎與廣俱，異道。

translations.literal: Three years later Guang, as superintendent of the palace gentlemen, led four thousand riders out of Youbeiping; the Marquis of Bowang Zhang Qian led ten thousand riders together with Guang, by a different route.
translations.idiomatic: Three years later he led four thousand horse out of Youbeiping in his capacity as superintendent of gentlemen, while the Marquis of Bowang Zhang Qian took ten thousand cavalry by another line of march to support him.

sentences.zh: 行數百里，匈奴左賢王將四萬騎圍廣，廣軍士皆恐，廣乃使其子敢往馳之。

translations.literal: Having gone several hundred li, the Xiongnu Worthy King of the Left led forty thousand riders to surround Guang; Guang's officers and soldiers were all afraid, and Guang then sent his son Gan to gallop against them.
translations.idiomatic: Several hundred li into the steppe the Worthy King of the Left ringed him with forty thousand horse. His men were terrified until Li Guang sent his son Li Gan to charge the encirclement.

sentences.zh: 敢從數十騎直貫胡騎，出其左右而還，報廣曰：「胡虜易與耳。」

translations.literal: Gan, with several dozen riders, went straight through the Hu riders, came out to their left and right and returned, reporting to Guang, "The Hu captives are easy to deal with."
translations.idiomatic: Li Gan took a few dozen troopers, punched straight through the enemy line, wheeled around their flanks, and rode back to report, "The barbarians are nothing much."

sentences.zh: 軍士乃安。

translations.literal: The officers and soldiers then were reassured.
translations.idiomatic: Only then did the ranks steady.

sentences.zh: 為圜陳外鄉，胡急擊，矢下如雨。

translations.literal: They made a circular formation facing outward; the Hu pressed the attack urgently; arrows fell like rain.
translations.idiomatic: He formed a hollow square facing outward; the Xiongnu pressed the assault and arrows fell in sheets.

sentences.zh: 漢兵死者過半，漢矢且盡。

translations.literal: More than half the Han soldiers died; the Han arrows were nearly exhausted.
translations.idiomatic: Casualties passed fifty percent and their quivers were almost empty.

sentences.zh: 廣乃令持滿毋發，而廣身自以大黃射其裨將，殺數人，胡虜益解。

translations.literal: Guang then ordered them to hold full draw and not release, while Guang himself with a great yellow crossbow shot their deputy generals, killing several men; the Hu captives gradually slackened.
translations.idiomatic: Li Guang ordered his men to hold at full draw without loosing while he himself worked a heavy crossbow and dropped several of their subordinate commanders; the pressure on the ring began to ease.

sentences.zh: 會暮，吏士無人色，而廣意氣自如，益治軍。

translations.literal: It happened to be dusk; the clerks and soldiers had no human color, but Guang's spirit was as of itself; he all the more put the army in order.
translations.idiomatic: Night came on; officers and men were grey-faced with exhaustion, but Li Guang was calm as ever and went about tightening the formation.

sentences.zh: 軍中服其勇也。

translations.literal: In the army they admired his bravery.
translations.idiomatic: The whole command admired his nerve.

sentences.zh: 明日，復力戰，而博望侯軍亦軍，匈奴乃解去。

translations.literal: The next day they again fought with full force, and the Marquis of Bowang's army also arrived as an army; the Xiongnu then broke off and departed.
translations.idiomatic: They fought again at daybreak, and when Zhang Qian's column finally came up the Xiongnu lifted the siege and withdrew.

sentences.zh: 漢軍邑，弗能追。

translations.literal: The Han army was low in spirit and could not pursue.
translations.idiomatic: Li Guang's men were too shaken and worn to follow the retreating enemy.

sentences.zh: 是時，廣軍幾沒，罷歸。

translations.literal: At this time Guang's army was nearly annihilated; they were dismissed and returned.
translations.idiomatic: His force had been all but wiped out; they were stood down and marched home.

sentences.zh: 漢法，博望侯後期，當死，贖為庶人。

translations.literal: By Han law the Marquis of Bowang was late to the rendezvous and should have died; he ransomed himself and became a commoner.
translations.idiomatic: Under military law Zhang Qian's late arrival was a capital offense; he bought off the sentence and was reduced to commoner status.

sentences.zh: 廣軍自當，亡賞。

translations.literal: Guang's army had borne the brunt itself; there was no reward.
translations.idiomatic: Li Guang had held the enemy alone and received no enfeoffment or bounty.

sentences.zh: 初，廣與從弟李蔡俱為郎，事文帝。

translations.literal: In the beginning Guang together with his cousin Li Cai were both gentlemen-attendants, serving Emperor Wen.
translations.idiomatic: In his youth Li Guang and his cousin Li Cai had both served as gentlemen-attendants under Emperor Wen.

sentences.zh: 景帝時，蔡積功至二千石。

translations.literal: In Emperor Jing's time Cai accumulated merit up to two-thousand-dan rank.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Jing, Li Cai rose through successive posts until he held a two-thousand-dan salary.

sentences.zh: 武帝元朔中，為輕車將軍，從大將軍擊右賢王，有功中率，封為樂安侯。

translations.literal: In the Yuanshuo period of Emperor Wu he was light-chariot general; following the supreme commander he struck the Worthy King of the Right, had merit meeting the quota, and was enfeoffed as Marquis of Le'an.
translations.idiomatic: In Emperor Wu's Yuanshuo years he led the light chariots under the supreme commander against the Worthy King of the Right, cleared the kill quota for enfeoffment, and was made Marquis of Le'an.

sentences.zh: 元狩二年，代公孫弘為丞相。

translations.literal: In the second year of Yuanshou he replaced Gongsun Hong as chancellor.
translations.idiomatic: In 121 BCE he succeeded Gongsun Hong as chancellor of the empire.

sentences.zh: 蔡為人在下中，名聲出廣下遠甚，然廣不得爵邑，官不過九卿。

translations.literal: Cai's person was in the lower middle grade; his fame fell far below Guang's, yet Guang did not obtain rank or fief; his office did not exceed the nine ministers.
translations.idiomatic: Li Cai was a mediocre man whose reputation could not compare with Li Guang's, yet it was Cai who won a marquisate while Li Guang never rose above the nine ministers and took no fief.

sentences.zh: 廣之軍吏及士卒或取封侯。

translations.literal: Among Guang's army clerks and soldiers some obtained enfeoffment as marquises.
translations.idiomatic: Some of Li Guang's own subalterns and privates had been ennobled for merit.

sentences.zh: 廣與望氣王朔語云：「自漢擊匈奴，廣未嘗不在其中，而諸妄校尉已下，材能不及中，以軍功取侯者數十人。

translations.literal: Guang spoke with Wang Shuo who read omens in the clouds, saying that since the Han struck the Xiongnu, Guang had never not been among them, yet of the various colonels and below, those whose talents and ability did not reach the middle grade—several tens of men had taken marquisates by military merit.
translations.idiomatic: Li Guang said to Wang Shuo, who read omens in the clouds, that he had fought in every Han campaign against the Xiongnu while dozens of colonels and men beneath them—talent no better than middling—had been ennobled for their service alone.

sentences.zh: 廣不為後人，然終無尺寸功以得封邑者，何也？

translations.literal: Guang is not behind others, yet in the end there is no merit even the size of a foot or an inch by which to obtain a fief—why is that?
translations.idiomatic: I have never lagged behind my peers, so why have I never earned even the smallest fief?

sentences.zh: 豈吾相不當侯邪？」

translations.literal: Could it be that my physiognomy is not suited to being a marquis?"
translations.idiomatic: Could it be I was never fated by my face to hold a marquisate?"

sentences.zh: 朔曰：「將軍自念，豈嘗有恨者乎？」

translations.literal: Shuo said, "General, reflect yourself—have you ever had something you regret?"
translations.idiomatic: Wang Shuo asked, "Think back, General—is there anything you still regret?"

sentences.zh: 廣曰：「吾為隴西守，羌嘗反，吾誘降者八百餘人，詐而同日殺之，至今恨獨此耳。」

translations.literal: Guang said, "When I was governor of Longxi, the Qiang once rebelled; I lured more than eight hundred who surrendered, deceived them, and killed them all the same day—to this day my regret is only this."
translations.idiomatic: Li Guang answered, "When I governed Longxi the Qiang rose; I persuaded more than eight hundred of them to lay down their arms, then broke faith and had every one executed the same day. That is the one act I cannot forget."

sentences.zh: 朔曰：「禍莫大於殺已降，此乃將軍所以不得侯者也。」

translations.literal: Shuo said, "No misfortune is greater than killing those who have already surrendered; this is why the general has not obtained a marquisate."
translations.idiomatic: Wang Shuo replied, "Nothing brings down a heavier curse than slaughtering men who have already yielded. That, General, is why the marquisate has never come."

sentences.zh: 廣歷七郡太守，前後四十餘年，得賞賜，輒分其戲下，飲食與士卒共之。

translations.literal: Guang passed through seven commanderies as governor; for more than forty years in all, whenever he obtained rewards or gifts he always divided them among those beneath his banner; drink and food he shared with officers and soldiers.
translations.idiomatic: He governed seven frontier commanderies over more than forty years; every imperial gift went to his men, and he ate and drank with the ranks as they did.

sentences.zh: 家無余財，終不言生產事。

translations.literal: His household had no surplus wealth; to the end he did not speak of matters of production.
translations.idiomatic: He died without savings and never bothered with land or trade.

sentences.zh: 為人長，爰臂，其善射亦天性，雖子孫他人學者莫能及。

translations.literal: In person he was tall, with long arms; his skill at archery was also inborn nature—though sons, grandsons, and others studied, none could match him.
translations.idiomatic: He was a tall man with long apelike arms, and his archery was a gift of nature that neither his heirs nor any pupil could duplicate.

sentences.zh: 廣吶口少言，與人居，則畫地為軍陳，射闊狹以飲。

translations.literal: Guang was slow of mouth and spoke little; when with others he would draw on the ground army formations and shoot at narrow and wide to determine drinking.
translations.idiomatic: He was a man of few words; in company he would sketch battle lines in the dust and wager drinks on contests of archery at narrow targets.

sentences.zh: 專以射為戲。

translations.literal: He devoted himself to taking archery as amusement.
translations.idiomatic: Archery was his only pastime.

sentences.zh: 將兵，乏絕處見水，士卒不盡飲，不近水；

translations.literal: When leading troops, in places of want and exhaustion when water was seen, if the soldiers had not all drunk, he did not approach the water;
translations.idiomatic: On campaign, if his men had not all drunk from a found water source, he would not touch it himself;

sentences.zh: 不盡餐，不嚐食；

translations.literal: if they had not all finished eating, he did not taste food;
translations.idiomatic: if they had not all been fed, he would not eat;

sentences.zh: 寬緩不苛，士以此愛樂為用。

translations.literal: he was lenient and easy, not harsh; by this the officers loved and delighted to be employed by him.
translations.idiomatic: yet his discipline was loose rather than cruel, and for that the troops loved him and fought willingly for him.

sentences.zh: 其射，見敵，非在數十步之內，度不中不發，發即應弦而倒。

translations.literal: As for his shooting, when he saw the enemy, if they were not within several tens of paces, he gauged that he would not hit and did not release; when he released, then they responded to the string and fell.
translations.idiomatic: He would not loose an arrow until the target stood within a few dozen paces and he was sure of the shot; when he did shoot, the man dropped with the twang of the bowstring.

sentences.zh: 用此，其將數困辱，及射猛獸，亦數為所傷雲。

translations.literal: By this means, as a general he several times met distress and humiliation; and in shooting fierce beasts, he also several times was wounded by them, it is said.
translations.idiomatic: That habit of closing to point-blank range cost him more than one battlefield setback, and the same boldness brought him repeated wounds from the beasts he hunted.

sentences.zh: 元狩四年，大將軍票騎將軍大擊匈奴，廣數自請行。

translations.literal: In the fourth year of Yuanshou the supreme commander and the swift-crossing general greatly struck the Xiongnu; Guang repeatedly requested permission to go.
translations.idiomatic: In 119 BCE the court launched the great double offensive under Wei Qing and Huo Qubing; Li Guang petitioned again and again to take the field.

sentences.zh: 上以為老，不許；

translations.literal: The emperor thought him old and did not grant permission;
translations.idiomatic: The emperor judged him too old and refused;

sentences.zh: 良久乃許之，以為前將軍。

translations.literal: after a long while he then granted it, making him vanguard general.
translations.idiomatic: after long insistence he relented and named him vanguard general.

sentences.zh: 大將軍青出塞，捕虜知單于所居，乃自以精兵走之，而令廣並於右將軍軍，出東道。

translations.literal: Supreme Commander Qing went beyond the barrier; capturing prisoners he learned where the Chanyu dwelt; then he himself with picked troops hastened thither, and ordered Guang to merge with the army of the general of the right, going out by the eastern road.
translations.idiomatic: Wei Qing crossed the frontier, learned from prisoners where the Chanyu lay, and drove for that point with his elite while ordering Li Guang to combine his column with the general of the right's and take the longer eastern route.

sentences.zh: 東道少回遠，大軍行，水草少，其勢不屯行。

translations.literal: The eastern road was somewhat circuitous and far; for a great army to march, grass and water were few; in its situation it could not halt and march.
translations.idiomatic: The eastern track swung wide, offered little forage or water, and could not support a large force moving in stages.

sentences.zh: 廣辭曰：「臣部為前將軍，今大將軍乃徙臣出東道，且臣結髮而與匈奴戰，乃令一得當單于，臣願居前，先死單于。」

translations.literal: Guang declined, saying, "My division was made vanguard general; now the supreme commander has shifted me out by the eastern road. Moreover, since I bound up my hair I have fought the Xiongnu; now when I might at last match the Chanyu, I wish to take the van and die confronting the Chanyu."
translations.idiomatic: Li Guang protested: "I was named vanguard, yet the commander-in-chief has shunted me onto the eastern track. I have fought the Xiongnu since I was a young man; now that I might finally meet the Chanyu in battle, I ask to lead the first line and strike him head-on."

sentences.zh: 大將軍陰受上指，以為李廣數奇，毋令當單于，恐不得所欲。

translations.literal: The supreme commander secretly received the emperor's pointer, thinking Li Guang's fortune repeatedly odd, not ordering him to face the Chanyu, fearing he would not obtain what was desired.
translations.idiomatic: Wei Qing had private orders from the throne: Li Guang's luck was thought ill-starred, so he must not be allowed to bear the brunt against the Chanyu lest the campaign miscarry.

sentences.zh: 是時，公孫敖新失侯，為中將軍，大將軍亦欲使敖與俱當單于，故徙廣。

translations.literal: At this time Gongsun Ao had newly lost his marquisate and was central general; the supreme commander also wished to make Ao together with him face the Chanyu, therefore shifted Guang.
translations.idiomatic: Gongsun Ao had just been stripped of his fief and was serving as central general; Wei Qing wanted him at his side for the strike on the Chanyu, which is why Li Guang was reassigned.

sentences.zh: 廣知之，固辭。

translations.literal: Guang knew it and firmly declined.
translations.idiomatic: Li Guang saw through the arrangement and refused again and again.

sentences.zh: 大將軍弗聽，令長史封書與廣之莫府，曰：「急詣部，如書。」

translations.literal: The supreme commander did not listen; he ordered the chief clerk to seal a letter and deliver it to Guang's headquarters, saying, "Hasten to your unit according to the letter."
translations.idiomatic: Wei Qing would not hear him out and sent his chief clerk with sealed orders to Li Guang's field office: "Report to your command at once—no argument."

sentences.zh: 廣不謝大將軍而起行，意象慍怒而就部，引兵與右將軍食其合軍出東道。

translations.literal: Guang did not thank the supreme commander but rose and went; his bearing seemed resentful and angry as he went to his unit; he led troops to join the army of General of the Right Shiqi and combined armies out the eastern road.
translations.idiomatic: Li Guang left without a word of thanks, his face dark with anger, took his post, and marched to join General of the Right Zhao Shiqi for the eastern approach.

sentences.zh: 惑失道，後大將軍。

translations.literal: Confused, they lost the way and lagged behind the supreme commander.
translations.idiomatic: They wandered off the route and arrived too late to support Wei Qing's main blow.

sentences.zh: 大將軍與單于接戰，單于遁走，弗能得而還。

translations.literal: The supreme commander joined battle with the Chanyu; the Chanyu fled; they could not catch him and returned.
translations.idiomatic: Wei Qing engaged the Chanyu, who broke off and escaped; the Han pursuit could not run him down.

sentences.zh: 南絕幕，乃遇兩將軍。

translations.literal: Going south they crossed the desert; then they met the two generals.
translations.idiomatic: On the march south across the Gobi they at last met Li Guang's and Zhao Shiqi's columns.

sentences.zh: 廣已見大將軍，還入軍。

translations.literal: Guang had already seen the supreme commander and returned into his army.
translations.idiomatic: After a brief interview with Wei Qing, Li Guang withdrew to his own camp.

sentences.zh: 大將軍使長史持糒醪遺廣，因問廣、食其失道狀，曰：「青欲上書報天子失軍曲折。」

translations.literal: The supreme commander sent the chief clerk bearing dried rations and ale to present to Guang, and thereupon asked Guang and Shiqi the circumstances of losing the way, saying, "Qing wishes to memorialize the Son of Heaven reporting the twists of the lost-army affair."
translations.idiomatic: Wei Qing sent his chief clerk with food and wine for Li Guang and questioned him and Zhao Shiqi about getting lost, adding that he meant to report the whole tangled affair to the emperor.

sentences.zh: 廣未對。

translations.literal: Guang did not reply.
translations.idiomatic: Li Guang said nothing.

sentences.zh: 大將軍長史急責廣之莫府上簿。

translations.literal: The supreme commander's chief clerk urgently pressed Guang's headquarters to submit the roster.
translations.idiomatic: The chief clerk then demanded that Li Guang's staff present him for the formal inquiry.

sentences.zh: 廣曰：「諸校尉亡罪，乃我自失道。

translations.literal: Guang said, "The various colonels are without guilt; it was I myself who lost the way.
translations.idiomatic: Li Guang said, "My colonels are blameless—the fault for losing the way is mine alone.

sentences.zh: 吾今自上簿。」

translations.literal: I will now go myself to submit the roster."
translations.idiomatic: I will go myself to make the deposition."

sentences.zh: 至莫府，謂其麾下曰：「廣結髮與匈奴大小七十餘戰，今幸從大將軍出接單于兵，而大將軍徙廣部行回遠，又迷失道，豈非天哉！

translations.literal: Reaching headquarters, he said to those beneath his banner, "Since I bound my hair I have fought the Xiongnu in more than seventy engagements, large and small; now, though I had the fortune to follow the supreme commander out against the Chanyu's host, the supreme commander shifted my unit onto a long detour, and I again lost the way—is this not Heaven!
translations.idiomatic: At his headquarters he told his officers, "Since I first took up arms I have fought the Xiongnu more than seventy times, large and small. I thought I would finally ride with the commander-in-chief against the Chanyu—only to be sent the long way round and lose the trail. Can this be anything but fate?

sentences.zh: 且廣年六十餘，終不能復對刀筆之吏矣！」

translations.literal: Moreover Guang is more than sixty; in the end I cannot again face clerks with knife and brush!"
translations.idiomatic: I am past sixty—I will not end my days answering to some clerk with his brush and knife!"

sentences.zh: 遂引刀自剄。

translations.literal: Thereupon he drew a knife and cut his own throat.
translations.idiomatic: He drew his sword and cut his own throat.

sentences.zh: 百姓聞之，知與不知，老壯皆為垂泣。

translations.literal: The hundred surnames hearing it, whether knowing or not knowing, old and young all for him shed tears.
translations.idiomatic: When word spread, everyone who heard it, whether they had known him or not, wept—old and young alike.

sentences.zh: 而右將軍獨下吏，當死，贖為遮人。

translations.literal: But the general of the right alone was handed to the officials; liable to death, he ransomed his sentence and was reduced to commoner status.
translations.idiomatic: General of the Right Zhao Shiqi alone was handed over to the judges, faced execution, and bought his life back at the price of commoner status.

sentences.zh: 廣三子，曰當戶、椒、敢，皆為郎。

translations.literal: Guang had three sons, called Danghu, Jiao, and Gan; all were gentlemen-attendants.
translations.idiomatic: Li Guang had three sons—Danghu, Jiao, and Gan—each of whom held a post as gentleman-attendant at court.

sentences.zh: 上與韓嫣戲，嫣少不遜，當戶擊嫣，嫣走，於是上以為能。

translations.literal: The emperor was sporting with Han Yan; Yan was somewhat insolent; Danghu struck Yan; Yan fled; thereupon the emperor thought him capable.
translations.idiomatic: Once when the emperor was joking with his favorite Han Yan, the man grew impertinent and Li Danghu struck him; Yan ran off, and the emperor judged the young man spirited.

sentences.zh: 當戶蚤死，乃拜椒為代郡太守，皆先廣死。

translations.literal: Danghu died early; then Jiao was appointed governor of Dai commandery; both died before Guang.
translations.idiomatic: Danghu died young; Jiao was later made governor of Dai. Both predeceased their father.

sentences.zh: 廣死軍中時，敢從票騎將軍。

translations.literal: When Guang died in the army, Gan was following the swift-crossing general.
translations.idiomatic: When Li Guang killed himself on campaign, Li Gan was serving under Huo Qubing.

sentences.zh: 廣死明年，李蔡以丞相坐詔賜冢地陽陵當得二十畝，蔡盜取三頃，頗賣得四十餘萬，又盜取神道外壖地一畝葬其中，當下獄，自殺。

translations.literal: The year after Guang died, Li Cai as chancellor was charged because by edict the tomb land granted at Yangling ought to have been twenty mu, yet Cai seized three qing, sold much of it for more than four hundred thousand cash, and also appropriated one mu of land beyond the spirit way for a burial—liable to imprisonment, he killed himself.
translations.idiomatic: The following year Chancellor Li Cai was charged with embezzling imperial tomb land at Yangling—twenty mu by decree, but he had seized three qing, sold off much of it for over four hundred thousand cash, and appropriated another mu beside the spirit way for a private burial. Facing prison, he took his own life.

sentences.zh: 敢以校尉從票騎將軍擊胡左賢王，力戰，奪左賢王旗鼓，斬首多，賜爵關內侯，食邑二百戶，代廣為郎中令。

translations.literal: Gan as colonel followed the swift-crossing general in striking the Hu Worthy King of the Left; he fought hard, seized the Worthy King of the Left's drums and banners, beheaded many, was granted rank as marquis within the passes with two hundred households' food income, and replaced Guang as superintendent of the palace gentlemen.
translations.idiomatic: Li Gan followed Huo Qubing as a colonel against the Worthy King of the Left, fought fiercely, captured the enemy drums and standards, piled up enough heads to be made marquis within the passes with an income of two hundred households, and succeeded his father as superintendent of gentlemen.

sentences.zh: 頃之，怨大將軍青之恨其父，乃擊傷大將軍，大將軍匿諱之。

translations.literal: Before long, resenting that Supreme Commander Qing had wronged his father, he then struck and wounded the supreme commander; the supreme commander concealed and hushed it.
translations.idiomatic: Soon afterward, nursing a grudge against Wei Qing for his father's death, he assaulted the commander-in-chief and wounded him; Wei Qing kept the matter quiet.

sentences.zh: 居無何，敢從上雍，至甘泉宮獵，票騎將軍去病怨敢傷青，射殺敢。

translations.literal: Before long had passed, Gan followed the emperor to Yong; reaching Ganquan Palace to hunt, the swift-crossing general Qu Bing resented that Gan had wounded Qing and shot and killed Gan.
translations.idiomatic: Not long after, while Li Gan was escorting the emperor on a hunt at Ganquan, Huo Qubing—still furious over the attack on his uncle—shot him dead.

sentences.zh: 去病時方貴幸，上為諱，云「鹿觸殺之」。

translations.literal: Qu Bing at the time was just honored and favored; the emperor for him covered it, saying "A deer gored him to death."
translations.idiomatic: Huo Qubing was then at the height of imperial favor, so the court passed off the killing as a hunting accident—a stag had gored Li Gan.

sentences.zh: 居歲餘，去病死。

translations.literal: After more than a year Qu Bing died of illness.
translations.idiomatic: A little over a year later Huo Qubing himself died of illness.

sentences.zh: 敢有女為太子中人，愛幸。

translations.literal: Gan had a daughter who was a palace attendant of the heir apparent and was loved and favored.
translations.idiomatic: Li Gan left a daughter who served as an attendant in the heir apparent's inner quarters and won the young prince's favor.

sentences.zh: 敢男禹有寵於太子，然好利，亦有勇。

translations.literal: Gan's son Yu had favor with the heir apparent, yet loved profit, and also had courage.
translations.idiomatic: His son Li Yu was a favorite of the heir apparent—bold, but greedy.

sentences.zh: 嘗與侍中貴人飲，侵陵之，莫敢應。

translations.literal: Once when drinking with palace-attendant nobles he encroached and bullied them; none dared respond.
translations.idiomatic: Once at wine with several high-ranking attendants he browbeat them until no one dared answer back.

sentences.zh: 後訴之上，上召禹，使刺虎，縣下圈中，未至地，有詔引出之。

translations.literal: Afterward they complained to the emperor; the emperor summoned Yu, ordered him to stab a tiger, suspended him down into the pen; before reaching the ground, there was an edict drawing him out.
translations.idiomatic: They complained to the throne; the emperor summoned Li Yu for a tiger hunt and had him lowered into the pit on ropes, then countermanded the drop and had him winched up before his feet touched the ground.

sentences.zh: 禹從落中以劍斫絕累，欲刺虎。

translations.literal: Yu, while being lowered, with a sword chopped and severed the ropes that bound him, intending to stab the tiger.
translations.idiomatic: Halfway down he cut himself free with his blade and made as if to fight the animal anyway.

sentences.zh: 上壯之，遂救止焉。

translations.literal: The emperor thought him stalwart and thereupon rescued and stopped it.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor admired his nerve and called off the test.

sentences.zh: 而當戶有遺腹子陵，將兵擊胡，兵敗，降匈奴。

translations.literal: But Danghu had a posthumous son Ling; leading troops he struck the Hu; the army was defeated and he surrendered to the Xiongnu.
translations.idiomatic: Danghu's posthumous son was Li Ling, who led troops against the Xiongnu, was defeated, and went over to the enemy.

sentences.zh: 後人告禹謀欲亡從陵，下吏死。

translations.literal: Afterward someone reported that Yu plotted to flee and join Ling; he was handed to officials and died.
translations.idiomatic: Later someone accused Li Yu of plotting to defect to Li Ling; he was arrested and died in custody.

sentences.zh: 陵字少卿，少為侍中建章監。

translations.literal: Ling's courtesy name was Shaoqing; in youth he was palace attendant and Jianzhang supervisor.
translations.idiomatic: Li Ling, courtesy name Shaoqing, began his career as palace attendant and supervisor of the Jianzhang Palace.

sentences.zh: 善騎射，愛人，謙讓下士，甚得名譽。

translations.literal: Skilled at riding and archery, loving men, yielding and humble toward officers, he obtained great reputation.
translations.idiomatic: He excelled in horsemanship and the bow, treated others generously, and was modest toward his juniors—qualities that won him wide esteem.

sentences.zh: 武帝以為有廣之風，使將八百騎，深入匈奴二千餘里，過居延視地形，不見虜，還。

translations.literal: Emperor Wu thought he had Guang's style, ordered him to lead eight hundred riders, penetrate deeply into the Xiongnu more than two thousand li, pass Juyan to survey terrain, saw no enemy, and returned.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor saw in him something of Li Guang's dash and sent him with eight hundred horse more than two thousand li into the steppe, beyond Juyan to map the ground. He encountered no Xiongnu and marched home.

sentences.zh: 拜為騎都尉，將勇敢五千人，教射酒泉、張掖以備胡。

translations.literal: He was appointed cavalry commandant, leading five thousand picked brave men, teaching archery at Jiuquan and Zhangye to prepare against the Hu.
translations.idiomatic: He was promoted to cavalry commandant with five thousand picked troops, drilling archery at Jiuquan and Zhangye against future raids.

sentences.zh: 數年，漢遣貳師將軍伐大宛，使陵將五校兵隨後。

translations.literal: Several years later the Han dispatched the Ershi general to attack Dayuan, ordering Ling to lead the five colonels' troops following behind.
translations.idiomatic: Some years afterward, when the Ershi general marched against Dayuan, Li Ling was ordered to follow with the five colonels' contingents.

sentences.zh: 行至塞，會貳師還。

translations.literal: When they reached the barrier, it happened that Ershi returned.
translations.idiomatic: He reached the frontier just as the Ershi column was returning.

sentences.zh: 上賜陵書，陵留吏士，與輕騎五百出敦煌，至鹽水，迎貳師還，復留屯張掖。

translations.literal: The emperor bestowed a letter on Ling; Ling retained clerks and soldiers, and with five hundred light riders went out of Dunhuang to the salt-lake region, welcomed Ershi's return, and again remained garrisoned at Zhangye.
translations.idiomatic: An edict from the emperor reached Li Ling: he was to hold his troops, take five hundred light cavalry from Dunhuang to the saline lakes to meet the returning Ershi force, then go back to his camp at Zhangye.

sentences.zh: 天漢二年，貳師將三萬騎出酒泉，擊右賢王於天山。

translations.literal: In the second year of Tianhan the Ershi general led thirty thousand riders out of Jiuquan and struck the Worthy King of the Right at the Celestial Mountains.
translations.idiomatic: In 99 BCE the Ershi general took thirty thousand cavalry from Jiuquan against the Worthy King of the Right on the Tian Shan range.

sentences.zh: 召陵，欲使為貳師將輜重。

translations.literal: He summoned Ling, wishing to make him for Ershi lead the baggage train.
translations.idiomatic: Li Ling was summoned to escort supplies for the Ershi army.

sentences.zh: 陵召見武台，叩頭自請曰：「臣所將屯邊者，皆荊楚勇士奇材劍客也，力扼虎，射命中，願得自當一隊，到蘭干山南以分單于兵，毋令專鄉貳師軍。」

translations.literal: Ling was summoned to audience at the martial terrace, knocked his head and requested of himself, saying, "Those whom your servant leads garrisoning the border are all Jing-Chu bravos, extraordinary talents and wandering swordsmen; their strength strangles tigers, their shooting hits the mark; I wish to obtain to command a separate unit myself, reaching south of Mount Langgan to divide the Chanyu's troops, not letting them concentrate against the Ershi army alone."
translations.idiomatic: Summoned to the Wu Terrace, Li Ling kowtowed and begged to lead his own detachment: the men he had trained on the frontier were Jing-Chu fighters who could strangle a tiger and never miss with the bow; let him march south of Mount Langgan to pin part of the Chanyu's host so it could not fall entirely on the Ershi column.

sentences.zh: 上曰：「將惡相屬邪！

translations.literal: The emperor said, "Do generals dislike being subordinate to one another!
translations.idiomatic: The emperor snapped, "So generals refuse to serve under one another!

sentences.zh: 吾發軍多，毋騎予女。」

translations.literal: I am dispatching many troops; I have no cavalry to give you."
translations.idiomatic: I have committed every spare mount—there are no cavalry left for you."

sentences.zh: 陵對：「無所事騎，臣願以少擊眾，步兵五千人涉單于庭。」

translations.literal: Ling replied, "There is no need to use cavalry; your servant wishes with the few to strike the many—five thousand foot soldiers to tread the Chanyu's court."
translations.idiomatic: Li Ling answered, "I do not need horse. Give me five thousand foot soldiers and I will march into the heart of the Chanyu's domain."

sentences.zh: 上壯而許之，因詔強弩都尉路博德將兵半道迎陵軍。

translations.literal: The emperor thought him stalwart and granted it, thereupon edicting crossbow commandant Lu Bode to lead troops halfway to meet Ling's army.
translations.idiomatic: Struck by his boldness, the emperor approved and ordered Lu Bode, commandant of strong crossbowmen, to move up the line and cover Li Ling's retreat.

sentences.zh: 博德故伏波將軍，亦羞為陵後距，奏言：「方秋匈奴馬肥，未可與戰，臣願留陵至春，俱將酒泉、張掖騎各五千人並擊東西浚稽，可必禽也。」

translations.literal: Bode had formerly been wave-subduing general; he also was ashamed to be Ling's rear guard, and memorialized, saying, "It is just autumn—the Xiongnu horses are fat; one cannot yet fight them; your servant wishes to detain Ling until spring, both leading Jiuquan and Zhangye riders five thousand each jointly to strike eastern and western Junji—then capture can be assured."
translations.idiomatic: Lu Bode, a former wave-subduing general, was mortified at playing rear guard to Li Ling and memorialized that autumn was the wrong season—the herds were fat; better to hold Li Ling until spring, then strike east and west Junji together with five thousand horse from Jiuquan and five thousand from Zhangye for a sure victory.

sentences.zh: 書奏，上怒，疑陵悔不欲出而教博德上書，乃詔博德：「吾欲予李陵騎，云『欲以少擊眾』。

translations.literal: When the memorial was presented the emperor was angry, suspected Ling regretted and did not wish to go out and had taught Bode to memorialize, and thereupon edicted Bode, "I wished to give Li Ling cavalry; he said 'I wish with the few to strike the many.'
translations.idiomatic: The emperor took the memorial as a conspiracy—Li Ling backing out and putting Lu Bode up to delay—and sent Lu Bode a blistering reply: "I had offered Li Ling cavalry; he insisted on attacking a host with a handful."

sentences.zh: 今虜入西河，其引兵走西河，遮鉤營之道。」

translations.literal: Now the enemy have entered Xihe; lead your troops in haste to Xihe and block the road to the Gouying encampment."
translations.idiomatic: The barbarians are in Xihe—march there at once and seal the approaches to the encampment at Gouying."

sentences.zh: 詔陵：「以九月發，出庶虜鄣，至東浚稽山南龍勒水上，徘徊觀虜，即亡所見，從浞野侯趙破奴故道抵受降城休士，因騎置以聞。

translations.literal: He edicted Ling: "Set out in the ninth month, exit the Zhelu barrier fort, reach the southern slopes of Eastern Junji Mountain on the Longle River, linger and observe the enemy; if you see nothing, follow Marquis Zhuoye Zhao Ponu's old route to Accept-Surrender City to rest the troops, then report by mounted courier relay."
translations.idiomatic: Li Ling was told: "Leave in the ninth month, pass the Zhelu barrier, and take position south of Eastern Junji on the Longle River to scout. If you find no sign of the enemy, follow Zhao Ponu's old track to Accept-Surrender City, rest the column, and send word by relay."

sentences.zh: 所與博德言者云何？

translations.literal: What of what you said with Bode?
translations.idiomatic: What passed between you and Lu Bode?

sentences.zh: 具以書對。」

translations.literal: Fully reply in writing."
translations.idiomatic: Answer every point in a written memorial."

sentences.zh: 陵於是將其步卒五千人出居延，北行三十日，至浚稽山止營，舉圖所過山川地形，使麾下騎陳步樂還以聞。

translations.literal: Ling thereupon led his foot soldiers five thousand men out of Juyan, marched north thirty days, reached Junji Mountain and halted camp, raised maps of the mountains and rivers and terrain passed through, and sent his subordinate rider Chen Bule to return and report.
translations.idiomatic: Li Ling took his five thousand foot out of Juyan, marched north for thirty days, and halted on Junji Mountain, where he charted every ridge and river. He sent his trooper Chen Bule back to court with the maps and a full report.

sentences.zh: 步樂召見，道陵將率得士死力，上甚說，拜步樂為郎。

translations.literal: Bule was summoned to audience; he described how Ling as general obtained the officers' death-effort; the emperor was very pleased and appointed Bule gentleman-attendant.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Bule was brought before the throne and described how Li Ling had inspired his men to fight to the death. The emperor was delighted and made Chen a gentleman-attendant.

sentences.zh: 陵至浚稽山，與單于相直，騎可三萬圍陵軍。

translations.literal: Ling reached Junji Mountain, confronted the Chanyu straight on, and about thirty thousand riders surrounded Ling's army.
translations.idiomatic: On Junji Mountain Li Ling ran head-on into the Chanyu; some thirty thousand horsemen closed a ring around his camp.

sentences.zh: 軍居兩山間，以大車為營。

translations.literal: The army dwelt between two mountains, using great carts as camp.
translations.idiomatic: He drew his wagons into a laager between two ridges.

sentences.zh: 陵引士出營外為陳，前行持戟盾，後行持弓弩，令曰：「聞鼓聲而縱，聞金聲而止。」

translations.literal: Ling led the men out beyond the camp to form battle array; the front ranks held halberds and shields, the rear ranks held bows and crossbows; he ordered, "Hear the drum and advance; hear the metal and halt."
translations.idiomatic: He marched his men outside the wagon ring: spearmen and shieldmen in front, archers behind, with the standing order—advance on the drum, halt at the gong.

sentences.zh: 虜見漢軍少，直前就營。

translations.literal: The captives saw the Han army was few and came straight forward toward the camp.
translations.idiomatic: The Xiongnu, seeing how small the Han detachment was, drove straight for the laager.

sentences.zh: 陵搏戰攻之，千弩俱發，應弦而倒。

translations.literal: Ling grapple-fought and attacked them; a thousand crossbows all released together; they responded to the string and fell.
translations.idiomatic: Li Ling met the charge; a thousand crossbows spoke at once and the enemy wave collapsed.

sentences.zh: 虜還走上山，漢軍追擊，殺數千人。

translations.literal: The captives ran back up the mountain; the Han army pursued and struck, killing several thousand men.
translations.idiomatic: The enemy fled uphill; Li Ling's men pursued and cut down several thousand.

sentences.zh: 單于大驚，召左右地兵八萬餘騎攻陵。

translations.literal: The Chanyu was greatly alarmed and summoned left- and right-district troops of more than eighty thousand riders to attack Ling.
translations.idiomatic: Alarmed, the Chanyu called in more than eighty thousand horse from his left and right wings to crush Li Ling.

sentences.zh: 陵且戰且引，南行數日，抵山谷中。

translations.literal: Ling while fighting withdrew, going south several days, reaching a mountain valley.
translations.idiomatic: Li Ling fought a fighting retreat southward for days until he reached a defile.

sentences.zh: 連戰，士卒中矢傷，三創者載輦，兩創者將車，一創者持兵戰。

translations.literal: In successive battles soldiers were hit by arrows and wounded; those with three wounds rode in carts, those with two drove the carts, those with one wound grasped weapons and fought.
translations.idiomatic: Battle followed battle; men with three wounds were carried in supply carts, those with two drove the wagons, the lightly wounded still stood in the line.

sentences.zh: 陵曰：「吾士氣少衰而鼓不起者，何也？

translations.literal: Ling said, "Our officers' spirit is somewhat declining yet the drums do not rise—why is that?
translations.idiomatic: Li Ling asked, "Our men are flagging, yet the drums will not carry—what is wrong?

sentences.zh: 軍中豈有女子乎？」

translations.literal: Could there be women in the army?"
translations.idiomatic: Are there women hidden in this column?"

sentences.zh: 始軍出時，關東群盜妻子徙邊者隨軍為卒妻婦，大匿車中。

translations.literal: When the army first set out, wives of Guandong bandit gangs who had been moved to the border followed the army as soldiers' wives and concubines, largely concealed in the carts.
translations.idiomatic: At the outset of the march the wives of exiled bandits from east of the passes had stowed away in the baggage train to live as soldiers' women.

sentences.zh: 陵搜得，皆劍斬之。

translations.literal: Ling searched them out and all with the sword beheaded them.
translations.idiomatic: Li Ling had them dragged out and executed every one at sword point.

sentences.zh: 明日復戰，斬首三千餘級。

translations.literal: The next day they fought again and took more than three thousand heads.
translations.idiomatic: The next day's fighting cost the enemy another three thousand dead.

sentences.zh: 引兵東南，循故龍城道行四五日，抵大澤葭葦中，虜從上風縱火，陵亦令軍中縱火以自救。

translations.literal: He led the troops southeast, following the old Dragon City road going four or five days, reaching a great marsh amid reeds; the captives from the upwind side set fire, and Ling also ordered the army to set fire to save itself.
translations.idiomatic: He struck southeast along the old Dragon City track for four or five days into a reed-choked marsh. The Xiongnu set the reeds alight upwind; Li Ling burned a ring of his own to keep the flames off his men.

sentences.zh: 南行至山下，單于在南山上，使其子將騎擊陵。

translations.literal: Going south they reached below a mountain; the Chanyu was on the southern mountain and sent his son leading riders to strike Ling.
translations.idiomatic: Farther south the Chanyu watched from a southern height and sent his son down at the head of cavalry.

sentences.zh: 陵軍步鬬樹木間，復殺數千人，因發連弩射單于，單于下走。

translations.literal: Ling's army foot-fought among the trees, again killing several thousand men; thereupon they released repeating crossbows to shoot the Chanyu, and the Chanyu went down and fled.
translations.idiomatic: Li Ling's infantry fought from tree to tree, killed thousands more, then volleyed repeating crossbows at the Chanyu and drove him from the ridge.

sentences.zh: 是日捕得虜，言：「單于曰：『此漢精兵，擊之不能下，日夜引吾南近塞，得毋有伏兵乎？』

translations.literal: That day they captured a prisoner who said, "The Chanyu said, 'These are Han picked troops; striking them we cannot bring them down; day and night they draw us south near the barrier—could there not be ambush troops?'"
translations.idiomatic: A prisoner taken that day reported the Chanyu's words: "These are Han crack troops—we cannot break them, yet they keep drawing us south toward the passes. Is there an ambush ahead?"

sentences.zh: 諸當戶君長皆言：『單于自將數萬騎擊漢數千人不能滅，後無以復使邊臣，令漢益輕匈奴。』

translations.literal: The various household heads and chiefs all said, 'The Chanyu himself leading several tens of thousands of riders cannot destroy several thousand Han—afterward there will be no way again to employ border officials; it will make the Han all the more despise the Xiongnu.'"
translations.idiomatic: The clan headmen replied: "If the Chanyu in person cannot wipe out a few thousand Han with his main host, he will never again command respect on the frontier—the Han will only despise us more."

sentences.zh: 復力戰山谷間，尚四五十里得平地，不能破，乃還。」

translations.literal: Fight again with full force in the mountain valleys; level ground still lies forty or fifty li ahead—if we cannot break them by then, withdraw."
translations.idiomatic: Keep pressing them in the defiles; the plain is still forty or fifty li off—if we cannot destroy them before that, we should break off and retire."

sentences.zh: 是時，陵軍益急，匈奴騎多，戰一日數十合，復傷殺虜二千餘人。

translations.literal: At this time Ling's army was pressed ever more urgently; the Xiongnu riders were many; in one day they fought several tens of engagements, again wounding and killing more than two thousand of the enemy.
translations.idiomatic: The pressure on Li Ling tightened: the Xiongnu horse swarmed the field, and in dozens of clashes that day his men cut down another two thousand of the enemy.

sentences.zh: 虜不利，欲去，會陵軍候管敢為校尉所辱，亡降匈奴，具言「陵軍無後救，射矢且盡，獨將軍麾下及成安侯校各八百人為前行，以黃與白為幟，當使精騎射之即破矣。」

translations.literal: The captives were disadvantaged and wished to withdraw, when it happened that Ling's army scout Guan Gan, having been insulted by a colonel, fled and surrendered to the Xiongnu, reporting in full, "Ling's army has no rear relief; their arrows are nearly exhausted; only the general's personal command and the Marquis of Cheng'an's colonel, eight hundred each as vanguard, use yellow and white as banners—if you send picked riders to shoot them, they will be broken at once."
translations.idiomatic: The Xiongnu were on the point of quitting when a Han scout named Guan Gan, humiliated by his colonel, defected and betrayed the whole situation: no reserves, arrows almost gone, Li Ling and Han Yannian each leading eight hundred elite in the van under yellow and white pennons—strike that screen with picked bowmen, he said, and the column would shatter.

sentences.zh: 成安侯者，穎川人，父韓千秋，故濟南相，奮擊南越戰死，武帝封子延年為侯，以校尉隨陵。

translations.literal: The Marquis of Cheng'an was a man of Yingchuan; his father Han Qianqiu, former chancellor of Jinan, struck hard against Southern Yue and died in battle; Emperor Wu enfeoffed the son Yannian as marquis; as colonel he followed Ling.
translations.idiomatic: Han Yannian, Marquis of Cheng'an, came from Yingchuan; his father Han Qianqiu had died fighting the Southern Yue, and the son had inherited the marquisate. He now served Li Ling as a colonel.

sentences.zh: 單于得敢大喜，使騎並攻漢軍，疾呼曰：「李陵、韓延年趣降！」

translations.literal: The Chanyu obtained Gan and was greatly pleased; he ordered riders jointly to attack the Han army, shouting urgently, "Li Ling, Han Yannian—hasten to surrender!"
translations.idiomatic: The Chanyu was jubilant. He threw every rider forward in a massed assault, shouting for Li Ling and Han Yannian to yield at once.

sentences.zh: 遂遮道急攻陵。

translations.literal: Thereupon they blocked the road and urgently attacked Ling.
translations.idiomatic: They sealed the defile and threw wave after wave at Li Ling.

sentences.zh: 陵居谷中，虜在山上，四面射，矢如雨下。

translations.literal: Ling dwelt in the valley; the captives were on the mountain; from four sides they shot; arrows fell like rain.
translations.idiomatic: Li Ling was trapped in the bottom of a gorge while the Xiongnu ringed the heights and poured arrows into the bowl.

sentences.zh: 漢軍南行，未至鞮汗山，一日五十萬矢皆盡，即棄車去。

translations.literal: The Han army went south; before reaching Di-han Mountain, in one day five hundred thousand arrows were all exhausted; then they abandoned the carts and departed.
translations.idiomatic: They fought southward toward Di-han Mountain until half a million shafts were gone in a single day; then they abandoned the wagons.

sentences.zh: 士尚三千餘人，徒斬車輻而持之，軍吏持尺刀，抵山入陿谷。

translations.literal: The soldiers still were more than three thousand; they merely chopped cart spokes and grasped them; army clerks grasped foot-long knives; they reached the mountain and entered a narrow valley.
translations.idiomatic: Barely three thousand men remained, armed with hacked-off wheel spokes while the officers clutched short swords; they clawed their way toward a narrow pass.

sentences.zh: 單于遮其後，乘隅下壘石，士卒多死，不得行。

translations.literal: The Chanyu blocked their rear; taking advantage of a corner they rolled down piled stones; many soldiers died; they could not advance.
translations.idiomatic: The Chanyu closed the rear of the column and rolled boulders from the cliffs; the dead piled up and the Han could not move.

sentences.zh: 昏後，陵便衣獨步出營，止左右：「毋隨我，丈夫一取單于耳！」

translations.literal: After dusk Ling in plain clothes alone walked out of the camp, stopping those at his sides, "Do not follow me—a man takes the Chanyu's ear in one go!"
translations.idiomatic: After dark Li Ling changed into civilian dress, slipped out of camp alone, and waved his men back: "Stay where you are—I mean to bring back the Chanyu's head myself."

sentences.zh: 良久，陵還，大息曰：「兵敗，死矣！」

translations.literal: After a long while Ling returned, greatly sighing, saying, "The army is defeated—we are dead!"
translations.idiomatic: He came back a long while later, breathing hard: "The fight is lost—we are finished."

sentences.zh: 軍吏或曰：「將軍威震匈奴，天命不遂，後求道徑還歸，如浞野侯為虜所得，後亡還，天子客遇之，況於將軍乎！」

translations.literal: An army clerk said, "The general's might shakes the Xiongnu; Heaven's mandate did not succeed; afterward seek a path and return—as when the Zhuoye Marquis was taken by the captives and afterward fled back, the Son of Heaven treated him as a guest—how much more the general!"
translations.idiomatic: One of his officers urged him on: "Your name still terrifies the Xiongnu. Heaven has simply turned its face; find a track home as Zhao Ponu did after capture—the emperor received him courteously. How much more would he welcome you?"

sentences.zh: 陵曰：「公止！

translations.literal: Ling said, "Sir, stop!
translations.idiomatic: Li Ling cut him short:

sentences.zh: 吾不死，非壯士也。」

translations.literal: If I do not die, I am not a stalwart man."
translations.idiomatic: A man who will not die now is no soldier at all."

sentences.zh: 於是盡斬旌旗，及珍寶埋地中，陵歎曰：「復得數十矢，足以脫矣。

translations.literal: Thereupon they utterly cut down the banners and flags, and precious things they buried in the ground; Ling sighed, saying, "If again we had several tens of arrows, it would suffice to escape.
translations.idiomatic: They hacked down the standards, buried what treasure they had, and Li Ling groaned, "Another sheaf of arrows and we might still break out.

sentences.zh: 今無兵復戰，天明坐受縛矣！

translations.literal: Now without arms to fight again, at daybreak we will sit and be bound!
translations.idiomatic: We have no weapons left—at dawn we sit here to be tied like sheep!

sentences.zh: 各鳥獸散，猶有得脫歸報天子者。」

translations.literal: Each scatter like birds and beasts—still there may be those who escape and return to report to the Son of Heaven."
translations.idiomatic: Scatter like birds and beasts—some of you may yet reach the emperor with word of what happened."

sentences.zh: 令軍士人持二升糒，一半冰，期至遮虜鄣者相待。

translations.literal: He ordered the soldiers each to hold two sheng of dried rations and half a block of ice, appointing that those who reached the Zhelu barrier should wait for one another.
translations.idiomatic: He issued each man two measures of dry rations and a lump of ice and told them to rally again at the Zhelu barrier if they could win through.

sentences.zh: 夜半時，擊鼓起士，鼓不鳴。

translations.literal: In the middle of the night they beat the drum to rouse the troops; the drum did not sound.
translations.idiomatic: At midnight he ordered the general alarm; the drum gave no answering roll.

sentences.zh: 陵與韓延年俱上馬，壯士從者十餘人。

translations.literal: Ling and Han Yannian together mounted horses; stalwart followers were more than ten men.
translations.idiomatic: Li Ling and Han Yannian vaulted into the saddle; a dozen picked men rode with them.

sentences.zh: 虜騎數千追之，韓延年戰死。

translations.literal: Several thousand Hu riders pursued them; Han Yannian died in battle.
translations.idiomatic: Thousands of enemy horse overtook them; Han Yannian fell fighting.

sentences.zh: 陵曰：「無面目報陛下！」

translations.literal: Ling said, "I have no face to report to His Majesty!"
translations.idiomatic: Li Ling cried, "I cannot face the emperor again!"

sentences.zh: 遂降。

translations.literal: Thereupon he surrendered.
translations.idiomatic: He yielded to the Xiongnu.

sentences.zh: 軍人分散，脫至塞者四百餘人。

translations.literal: The soldiers scattered apart; those who escaped and reached the barrier were more than four hundred.
translations.idiomatic: The command broke and ran; a little over four hundred men won through to the frontier.

sentences.zh: 陵敗處去塞百餘里，邊塞以聞。

translations.literal: The place where Ling was defeated was more than a hundred li from the barrier; the border garrisons reported it.
translations.idiomatic: The field where Li Ling went down lay only a hundred-odd li from the passes, and couriers soon brought word to court.

sentences.zh: 上欲陵死戰，召陵母及婦，使相者視之，無死喪色。

translations.literal: The emperor wished Ling to fight to the death; he summoned Ling's mother and wife and had physiognomists examine them—they showed no color of death or mourning.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor had wanted Li Ling to fight to the finish; he even called in Li Ling's mother and wife for face-readers to inspect, and neither bore the marks of imminent bereavement.

sentences.zh: 後聞陵降，上怒甚，責問陳步樂，步樂自殺。

translations.literal: Later hearing Ling had surrendered, the emperor was greatly angry, harshly questioned Chen Bule, and Bule killed himself.
translations.idiomatic: When the news of surrender came, the emperor's fury fell on Chen Bule, who had praised Li Ling's troops; under interrogation he took his own life.

sentences.zh: 群臣皆罪陵，上以問太史令司馬遷，遷盛言：「陵事親孝，與士信，常奮不顧身以殉國家之急。

translations.literal: The various ministers all blamed Ling; the emperor thereupon asked Grand Astrologer Sima Qian, and Qian spoke at length, saying, "Ling served his parents with filial piety, with officers is trustworthy, and constantly exerted himself without regard for his person to meet the state's urgent need.
translations.idiomatic: The whole court denounced Li Ling, so the emperor turned to Sima Qian, who spoke up at length: Li Ling had been a dutiful son, a commander his men trusted, a man who had repeatedly risked his life for the realm.

sentences.zh: 其素所畜積也，有國士之風。

translations.literal: What he has long stored up within him has the manner of a knight of the state.
translations.idiomatic: That was the man he had always been—a true knight of the empire.

sentences.zh: 今舉事一不幸，全軀保妻子之臣隨而媒糵其短，誠可痛也！

translations.literal: Now one mischance in the undertaking, and ministers who preserve their persons and protect wives and children follow brewing his faults—truly it is lamentable!
translations.idiomatic: One reverse, and every courtier who cares only for his own skin and his family is ready to brew slander against him—it is shameful.

sentences.zh: 且陵提步卒不滿五千，深輮戎馬之地，抑數萬之師，虜救死扶傷不暇，悉舉引弓之民共攻圍之。

translations.literal: Moreover Ling led fewer than five thousand foot soldiers deep into country dominated by enemy horse, checked a host of tens of thousands; the captives had no leisure to tend their wounded and dead, and mustered every bowman of the realm to besiege him.
translations.idiomatic: Consider: he took fewer than five thousand foot deep into cavalry country, held off tens of thousands, and kept the enemy so busy tending their wounded that the Chanyu had to call up every archer on the steppe to ring him in.

sentences.zh: 轉鬪千里，矢盡道窮，士張空拳，冒白刃，北首爭死敵，得人之死力，雖古名將不過也。

translations.literal: Turning and fighting a thousand li, arrows exhausted and the road exhausted, soldiers stretched empty fists, braved white blades, north-facing contended to die against the foe—obtaining men's death-effort—even ancient famous generals did not surpass this.
translations.idiomatic: They fought a retreat of a thousand li until shafts and road alike gave out; his men faced naked steel with bare hands, turning north to die on the enemy—such devotion would have honored any general in history.

sentences.zh: 身雖䧟敗，然其所摧敗亦足暴於天下。

translations.literal: Though his person met defeat and ruin, yet what he crushed and defeated is also sufficient to display to the world.
translations.idiomatic: Though Li Ling himself was broken, the damage he dealt the Xiongnu was enough to resound across the realm.

sentences.zh: 彼之不死，宜欲得當以報漢也。」

translations.literal: His not choosing death now likely means he waits for the right moment to repay the Han."
translations.idiomatic: His staying alive probably means he hopes for another chance to serve the empire."

sentences.zh: 初，上遣貳師大軍出，財令陵為助兵，及陵與單于相值，而貳師功少。

translations.literal: In the beginning, when the emperor dispatched the Ershi great army out, he only ordered Ling as auxiliary troops; when Ling confronted the Chanyu straight on, yet Ershi's merit was small.
translations.idiomatic: At first the emperor had meant Li Ling only to support the main Ershi expedition; when Li Ling met the Chanyu in person, the Ershi column had little to show for itself.

sentences.zh: 上以遷誣罔，欲沮貳師，為陵遊說，下遷腐刑。

translations.literal: The emperor thought Qian deceived and falsely accused, wishing to thwart Ershi, pleading for Ling, and handed Qian down to mutilating punishment.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor read Sima Qian's defense as slander meant to undermine the Ershi general and cover for Li Ling; Sima Qian was sentenced to castration.

sentences.zh: 久之，上悔陵無救，曰：「陵當發出塞，乃詔強弩都尉令迎軍。

translations.literal: After a long time the emperor regretted that Ling had no rescue, saying, "When Ling ought to have set out beyond the barrier, then I edicted the crossbow commandant to order meeting the army.
translations.idiomatic: In time the emperor regretted leaving Li Ling unsupported: "He was already beyond the passes when I ordered the commandant of strong crossbowmen to move up and cover his retreat.

sentences.zh: 坐預詔之，得令老將生奸詐。」

translations.literal: Because I pre-edicted him, it made the old general produce deceit."
translations.idiomatic: By issuing that order too early I only gave an old fox room for intrigue."

sentences.zh: 乃遣使勞賜陵餘軍得脫者。

translations.literal: Thereupon he dispatched envoys to console and bestow gifts on those of Ling's remaining army who had escaped.
translations.idiomatic: He then sent envoys with rewards to the survivors of Li Ling's command who had reached Han soil.

sentences.zh: 陵在匈奴歲餘，上遣因杅將軍公孫敖將兵深入匈奴迎陵。

translations.literal: Ling had been among the Xiongnu more than a year when the emperor dispatched General Yangan Gongsun Ao to lead troops deep into the Xiongnu to welcome Ling.
translations.idiomatic: After Li Ling had spent a year with the Xiongnu, the emperor sent Gongsun Ao, general of Yangan, on a deep raid to bring him home.

sentences.zh: 敖軍無功還，曰：「捕得生口，言李陵教單于為兵以備漢軍，故臣無所得。」

translations.literal: Ao's army returned without merit, saying, "We captured a prisoner who said Li Ling teaches the Chanyu to drill troops against the Han army; therefore your servant obtained nothing."
translations.idiomatic: Gongsun Ao came back empty-handed and reported that prisoners claimed Li Ling was training Xiongnu troops for war on Han—which was why he had found no trace of him.

sentences.zh: 上聞，於是族陵家，母弟妻子皆伏誅。

translations.literal: The emperor hearing this, thereupon exterminated Ling's clan; mother, younger brothers, wife, and children all submitted to execution.
translations.idiomatic: Believing it, the emperor wiped out Li Ling's entire family—mother, brothers, wife, and children all executed.

sentences.zh: 隴西士大夫以李氏為愧。

translations.literal: The gentlemen and great families of Longxi took the Li surname as a shame.
translations.idiomatic: The gentry of Longxi were ashamed to bear the name Li.

sentences.zh: 其後，漢遣使使匈奴，陵謂使者曰：「吾為漢將步卒五千人橫行匈奴，以亡救而敗，何負於漢而誅吾家？」

translations.literal: Afterward the Han dispatched an envoy to the Xiongnu; Ling said to the envoy, "I for the Han led foot soldiers five thousand who ranged across the Xiongnu; because there was no rescue I was defeated—what fault toward the Han that my family was executed?"
translations.idiomatic: Later, when a Han envoy reached the steppe, Li Ling confronted him: "I led five thousand foot for the empire deep in Xiongnu territory and lost only because no relief came. What crime did my family commit?"

sentences.zh: 使者曰：「漢聞李少卿教匈奴為兵。」

translations.literal: The envoy said, "The Han heard that Li Shaoqing teaches the Xiongnu to drill troops."
translations.idiomatic: The envoy answered, "Court rumor said Li Shaoqing was drilling Xiongnu soldiers."

sentences.zh: 陵曰：「乃李緒，非我也。」

translations.literal: Ling said, "That was Li Xu—not I."
translations.idiomatic: Li Ling replied, "That was another man, Li Xu—not me."

sentences.zh: 李緒本漢塞外都尉，居奚侯城，匈奴攻之，緒降，而單于客遇緒，常坐陵上。

translations.literal: Li Xu had originally been Han frontier commandant beyond the barrier, dwelling at Xihou city; the Xiongnu attacked it, Xu surrendered, and the Chanyu treated Xu as a guest, always seating him above Ling.
translations.idiomatic: Li Xu had been a Han frontier commandant at Xihou; when the Xiongnu took the place he surrendered and won such favor that the Chanyu seated him above Li Ling at feasts.

sentences.zh: 陵痛其家以李緒而誅，使人刺殺緒。

translations.literal: Ling grieved that his family because of Li Xu was executed; he sent men to stab and kill Xu.
translations.idiomatic: Li Ling blamed Li Xu for the slaughter of his kin and had him assassinated.

sentences.zh: 大閼氏欲殺陵，單于匿之北方，大閼氏死乃還。

translations.literal: The great Lady wished to kill Ling; the Chanyu hid him in the north; when the great Lady died he then returned.
translations.idiomatic: The Chanyu's chief wife wanted Li Ling dead; the Chanyu hid him in the far north until she died and he could return.

sentences.zh: 單于壯陵，以女妻之，立為右校王，衛律為丁靈王，皆貴用事。

translations.literal: The Chanyu thought Ling stalwart, gave him a daughter as wife, established him as king of the right commandant, Wei Lu as king of the Dingling—both were honored and wielded power.
translations.idiomatic: The Chanyu admired him, married a daughter to him, and created him king of the right commandant; Wei Lu was made king of the Dingling—both rose to high office at court.

sentences.zh: 衛律者，父本長水胡人。

translations.literal: As for Wei Lu, his father was originally a Hu man of Changshui.
translations.idiomatic: Wei Lu's father had been a Hu tribesman enrolled in the Changshui corps.
